item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploitation, Huione Seizure, and Bashe’s Fake Hargreaves Lansdown Claim
item.content:encoded:
Cisco Unified CM Flaw Exploited After PoC Reveals File-Write Path to Root — Must Read

Threat actors are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical Cisco Unified CM flaw that can allow unauthenticated file writes and potential root escalation when the WebDialer service is enabled. Cisco has patched the issue in Unified CM 14SU6 and 15SU5, and organizations should update or disable WebDialer immediately.

  • Threat actors have begun actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical flaw affecting Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition.
  • CVE-2026-20230 is an improper input validation issue in specific HTTP requests that can let an unauthenticated remote attacker conduct server-side request forgery through an affected device.
  • Cisco said successful exploitation could allow an attacker to write files to the underlying operating system that could later be used to elevate to root.
  • Successful exploitation requires the Cisco WebDialer Web Service to be enabled, and the service is disabled by default.
  • Cisco patched the vulnerability in Unified CM and Unified CM SME versions 14SU6 and 15SU5, and advises disabling WebDialer if immediate patching is not possible.

DoJ Seizes Huione Cloud Account Tied to Cyber Scam Money Laundering — Should Read

The U.S. Justice Department seized a cloud account used by Huione Group subsidiaries to support crypto money laundering infrastructure tied to Southeast Asian scam operations, while Treasury expanded actions against related Huione successors and Prince Group entities. Authorities say the ecosystem helped move billions in fraud proceeds and continues to adapt despite enforcement.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice announced the seizure of a cloud computing account used by subsidiaries of Cambodia-based HuiOne Group as part of action against alleged cyber scam money laundering infrastructure.
  • The seized account hosted backend infrastructure for HuiOne subsidiaries, including HuiOne Guarantee, also known as Haowang Guarantee, which operated an illicit Telegram-based marketplace involved in billions of dollars in transactions between 2021 and 2025.
  • The Justice Department said HuiOne Guarantee provided escrow services for criminals and helped move funds stolen by Southeast Asian scam centers.
  • FinCEN assessed H-Pay Service PLC as a primary money laundering concern to guard against HuiOne Group’s attempts to circumvent being cut off from the U.S. financial system.
  • The U.S. Treasury also sanctioned nine individuals and 26 entities linked to Prince Group, which it had previously classified as a Transnational Criminal Organization tied to scam compounds, fraud, and money laundering.

UK Cybercrime Journal: Hargreaves Landsdown Extortion Attempt by Bashe — Should Read

Bashe/APT73 claimed it stole a Hargreaves Lansdown customer database, but analysis suggests the breach was fabricated using recycled data from older leaks. The key takeaway is that threat actors may exploit recent IT outages and customer anxiety to create believable but false extortion narratives.

  • Hargreaves Lansdown experienced customer-facing IT issues in September 2025 and March 2026, but the company said the incidents were temporary technical problems and that customer assets and data were secure.
  • On 27 April 2026, Bashe listed hl.co.uk on its Tor data leak site and claimed to have stolen a customer database containing 658,259 unique users.
  • On 8 May 2026, alleged Hargreaves Lansdown data appeared on DarkForums, with a sample containing addresses, names, emails, phone numbers, and dates of birth but no customer account numbers or transaction details.
  • Analysis of the posted sample found the email addresses appeared in older Verifications.io and People Data Labs breaches, which the article says likely indicates the Bashe and DarkForums data was fake.
  • The article assesses with high confidence that the alleged Hargreaves Lansdown breach was fabricated and that Bashe used recent outages and glitches to support a false extortion narrative.

item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploited, FortiBleed Harvests Credentials, macOS ClickFix Pushes AMOS
item.content:encoded:
FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation — Must Read

A Russian-speaking initial access broker is linked to FortiBleed, a large credential-harvesting campaign that compromised FortiGate firewalls and other internet-facing systems to collect more than 110 million credentials. The operation used mass scanning, brute forcing, and a custom FortigateSniffer tool to steal cleartext credentials and hashes for resale, lateral movement, and further intrusions.

  • A Russian-speaking initial access broker is assessed to be behind FortiBleed, a large-scale credential-harvesting operation that has targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally since February 2026.
  • The campaign collects credential lists, searches for exposed services, brute-forces accessible systems, and deploys bespoke sniffers on compromised firewalls to capture cleartext and hashed credentials.
  • A Golang-based tool called FortigateSniffer uses the FortiOS built-in diagnostic command “diagnose sniffer packet” to passively capture authentication traffic across 24 protocols from compromised appliances.
  • SOCRadar said FortiBleed appears to be part of a broader multi-vendor initial access operation targeting Fortinet devices, Synology NAS, Sophos firewalls, RDWeb portals, Citrix SSL-VPNs, and MS-SQL servers.
  • The attackers are estimated to have launched at least 659 credential-harvesting pipelines on May 31 and June 15, 2026, identifying more than 110 million credentials including RADIUS credentials, NTLM hashes, Kerberos hashes, and MySQL authentication tokens.

Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Cisco Unified Communications Manager flaw CVE-2026-20230 is now being exploited, with attackers able to abuse an SSRF bug to write files that can lead to root-level compromise. Cisco patched the issue on June 3, and defenders should update urgently as public exploit details are now available.

  • Cisco Unified Communications Manager and Unified Communications Manager Session Management Edition are affected by CVE-2026-20230, a high-severity server-side request forgery flaw.
  • Cisco released security updates for CVE-2026-20230 on June 3 and warned that successful exploitation could allow attackers to write files that could later be used to gain root privileges.
  • Defused reported that CVE-2026-20230 is now being actively exploited from a single IP address using file:// payloads to create files on affected devices.
  • The observed proof-of-concept activity attempted to write a text file named /tmp/cve-2026-20230-test.txt, suggesting the current exploitation is reconnaissance in nature.
  • SSD Secure published a technical write-up and proof-of-concept exploit showing how Webdialer URL handling can be abused to write arbitrary files and achieve remote code execution.

Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people — Must Read

Xsolis says a targeted phishing attack gave attackers access to files containing sensitive personal and health information for nearly 1.4 million people. The company has contained the incident, reset passwords, added security measures, and is offering affected individuals 12 months of identity monitoring through Kroll.

  • Xsolis says a targeted phishing attack on January 20, 2026 led to unauthorized activity in a limited portion of its environment that it detected on January 22, 2026.
  • The attackers accessed certain files containing customer information, including names, addresses, dates of birth, health insurance information, Social Security numbers, and medical treatment information.
  • Data provided to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services indicates that 1,396,519 people are impacted by the incident.
  • Xsolis says it contained the activity, launched an investigation with external cybersecurity experts, reported the incident to law enforcement, and is notifying potentially affected individuals by mail.
  • The company says it reset passwords for all users and key accounts, increased system monitoring, strengthened credential management, accelerated employee security training, and is offering 12 months of identity monitoring and identity theft restoration services through Kroll.

New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer — Should Read

A new macOS ClickFix campaign tricks users with fake CAPTCHA pages into pasting Terminal commands that silently download, mount, and launch DMG-based Atomic macOS Stealer malware. The malware steals passwords, browser data, crypto wallets, Keychain files, messaging app data, and documents, so users should never run website-provided Terminal commands they do not fully understand.

  • Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 discovered a macOS ClickFix campaign that uses fake CAPTCHA pages to instruct users to open Terminal and paste a malicious command.
  • The command downloads a DMG file from an attacker-controlled server, silently mounts it with macOS hdiutil, searches for an .app or .pkg, and launches it with the macOS open command.
  • The campaign delivers Atomic macOS Stealer, which steals browser credentials, browser history, authentication tokens, cryptocurrency wallet data, Apple Keychain database files, messaging app data, Apple Notes databases, and user documents.
  • The malware displays a fake System Preferences authentication prompt to trick the user into entering their password.
  • Palo Alto observed command-and-control servers at svs-verificationdate[.]beer and 196.251.107[.]171.

VA OIG Identifies Lack of Oversight of VA GenAI Chat Tools — Should Read

A VA OIG review found that Department of Veterans Affairs staff use of generative AI chat tools lacks sufficient safeguards and oversight, creating potential patient safety and sensitive data exposure risks. Health-ISAC points healthcare organizations to AI governance guidance to help manage these risks.

  • A review of Department of Veterans Affairs staff use of generative artificial intelligence tools identified potential patient safety risks from a lack of safeguards and oversight.
  • Health-ISAC said concern is growing that sensitive patient data may be shared with publicly accessible chatbots as generative AI tools are used more widely in healthcare.
  • Health-ISAC said AI tools could generate output that puts patients at risk of harm or even death.
  • Health-ISAC and the Health Sector Coordinating Council Cybersecurity Working Group issued guidance earlier this year on developing effective AI governance frameworks.
  • The article directs readers to Health-ISAC’s white paper, “Policies and Safeguards for a Safe Use of AI,” and the HSCC Health Industry AI Cyber Governance Framework Implementation Guide.

The post-quantum EO is an important milestone. Now it’s time to get to work — Should Read

Cloudflare welcomes the new U.S. executive order setting 2030–2031 deadlines for federal post-quantum cryptography migration, but argues agencies and vendors must start now, especially on public Internet traffic and authentication. The key takeaway is that post-quantum readiness cannot wait for full inventories or future guidance because harvest-now-decrypt-later risks already exist.

  • President Trump signed Executive Order 14409 on June 22, 2026, setting deadlines of December 31, 2030, for federal agencies to transition their most sensitive systems to post-quantum encryption and December 31, 2031, for post-quantum authentication.
  • The order applies binding deadlines to federal High Value Assets and high impact systems, while National Security Systems are excluded because they are on a separate NSA-managed track with deadlines between 2030 and 2033.
  • The article says post-quantum encryption is needed now to prevent attackers from collecting encrypted traffic today and decrypting it later once powerful quantum computers are available.
  • The article argues that post-quantum authentication is a larger migration challenge because it requires coordinated upgrades across clients, servers, certificate authorities, certificate transparency logs, root stores, and browsers.
  • Cloudflare recommends that organizations protect public Internet traffic now, update procurement requirements to demand post-quantum support, use a quantum impact inventory to prioritize migration, and begin planning for post-quantum authentication.

Five Eyes agencies sound alarm about AI’s threat to cybersecurity — May Read

Five Eyes cyber agencies warned that frontier AI could transform offensive and defensive cyber capabilities within months, shrinking the time between vulnerability discovery and exploitation. They urged business leaders to urgently reduce attack surfaces, harden access controls, speed patching, retire legacy systems, and strengthen cyber resilience.

  • Five Eyes intelligence agencies warned that rapidly evolving AI is an urgent cybersecurity threat that business leaders must address immediately.
  • The agencies said frontier AI models could exceed current industry expectations within months and transform both offensive and defensive cyber capabilities.
  • The announcement said AI lowers barriers for malicious actors, increases the speed and complexity of attacks, and shrinks the window between vulnerability discovery and exploitation.
  • The agencies urged leaders to reduce attack surfaces, limit unnecessary access and external connectivity, speed up patching, address unsupported legacy systems, and strengthen access controls.
  • The announcement recommended that business leaders fund and empower internal cyber leaders, follow evolving guidance, adopt secure-by-design and secure-by-default approaches, and maintain defense in depth.

Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature — May Read

Microsoft’s optional Windows 11 KB5095093 preview update for versions 24H2 and 25H2 begins rolling out a new Point-in-Time Restore feature that can roll back the full PC state using recent restore points. The update also includes many fixes and improvements, including a Recycle Bin filename bug fix, though a known issue with third-party apps launching Office documents remains.

  • Microsoft released the optional KB5095093 preview cumulative update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2 as part of its monthly non-security preview update schedule.
  • The update begins rolling out Point-in-Time Restore, which lets users restore a Windows PC, including apps, settings, and personal files, to a recent automatic restore point.
  • For consumers, Point-in-Time Restore creates new restore points every 24 hours and deletes them after 72 hours or when allocated storage space runs out.
  • KB5095093 fixes a known Recycle Bin issue where the confirmation dialog could show an internal file name instead of the original file name when permanently deleting a file.
  • Microsoft says a known issue remains where third-party applications may be unable to launch Microsoft Office applications or open documents, and affected users should open the application or document directly.

Silent Push partners with CDL Cyber Intelligence to bring preemptive defense to the United Kingdom — Optional

Silent Push has partnered with UK-based CDL Cyber Intelligence to offer preemptive threat intelligence that identifies attacker infrastructure before campaigns reach targets. The partnership focuses on giving UK organizations earlier warning against state-linked and other threats using Silent Push’s Context Graph and Traffic Origin capabilities.

  • Silent Push has entered a strategic partnership with CDL Cyber Intelligence, an intelligence-led cybersecurity firm serving organisations across the UK and Europe.
  • CDL Cyber Intelligence’s team includes former intelligence officers, ethical hackers, and security engineers with experience in threat intelligence, incident response, attack surface management, and dark web monitoring.
  • Silent Push says its Context Graph tracks infrastructure creation and connections across DNS, WHOIS, certificates, and hosting data to surface Indicators of Future Attack before campaigns reach targets.
  • The article states that CDL’s clients get an average of 157 days of lead time before a campaign reaches their perimeter.
  • Silent Push says its Traffic Origin capability identifies the true upstream country of origin controlling a connection, even when the observed IP appears clean.

item.title: Federal PQC deadlines, Scattered Spider plea, and AI agent supply-chain risks
item.content:encoded:
Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London — Should Read

Two alleged Scattered Spider members pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London in 2024, an attack that stole customer data, disrupted services, and caused about £29 million in losses. The case highlights the major operational and financial impact cyber intrusions can have on critical public infrastructure.

  • Two members of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London systems in 2024 after previously denying involvement.
  • The intrusion took place between August 31 and September 3, 2024, and disrupted TfL services, including delaying customer refunds after attackers accessed data from the Oyster refunds system.
  • Transport for London said customer data was stolen in the attack, and the National Crime Agency said the incident caused £29 million in financial damage.
  • According to the NCA, the attack forced all 28,000 TfL employees to visit their local offices to reset their passwords.
  • Investigators said they found evidence on devices seized from Flowers’ home, including a screenshot showing connectivity to TfL infrastructure and videos showing Jubair breaching TfL systems.

Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents — Should Read

A fake AI agent skill passed multiple popular security scanners, was added to a trusted marketplace, and reportedly spread to thousands of agents by hiding its real instructions behind an external link that was changed after approval. The key takeaway is that current skill-scanning and trust signals like GitHub stars do not reliably protect AI agents from malicious or mutable external dependencies.

  • Security firm AIR created a fake AI agent skill called brand-landingpage, submitted it to a popular skill marketplace, promoted it with an Instagram ad, and says it reached about 26,000 agents, including some on corporate accounts.
  • AIR reported that every skill security scanner it tested, including Cisco's skill-scanner, NVIDIA's Skillspector, and the scanners used by skills.sh, marked the skill as safe.
  • The skill itself contained no harmful setup steps and instead instructed the agent to install a Stitch SDK by visiting stitch-design.ai, a domain controlled by AIR rather than Google.
  • After the skill had been widely installed, AIR changed the external page so it told the agent to download and run a script that, in the demonstration, only sent the user's email address back to AIR.
  • The article says the core weakness is structural because scanners review a fixed submitted package, while the external web content a skill points to can be changed after approval.

Payouts King Ransomware Initial Access Broker Deploys New Edgecution Malware — Should Read

Zscaler uncovered Edgecution, a new malware campaign tied to a Payouts King initial access broker that uses a malicious Microsoft Edge extension and Chrome native messaging to bypass the browser sandbox and run a Python backdoor on victims’ systems. The key takeaway is that attackers are combining Teams-based social engineering with a stealthy, headless-browser extension to gain persistent host access and execute commands while evading many traditional defenses.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz analyzed a campaign linked to an initial access broker associated with Payouts King ransomware that deploys a malicious Microsoft Edge extension called Edgecution.
  • The campaign begins with social engineering through Microsoft Teams messages and a fake Microsoft update website that delivers setup scripts, an AutoHotKey executable, and an encrypted ZIP archive.
  • Edgecution uses the Chrome native messaging protocol to let a hidden Microsoft Edge extension communicate with a Python-based backdoor that can collect system information, access the filesystem, launch processes, and execute arbitrary code.
  • The setup creates a scheduled task that launches Microsoft Edge in headless mode with the malicious extension loaded, making the extension invisible during normal browser use.
  • ThreatLabz observed Edgecution command-and-control servers communicating over WebSockets on cloudfront.net subdomains hosted on Amazon Web Services.

Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration — Should Read

President Trump signed an executive order requiring U.S. federal agencies to migrate high-value and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography by 2030 for key establishment and 2031 for digital signatures, accelerating the government’s previous timeline. The order also pushes agencies and contractors to inventory where current cryptography is used so they can replace vulnerable algorithms before quantum computers make today’s encrypted data readable.

  • President Trump signed an executive order on June 22 requiring federal agencies to move high-value assets and high-impact systems to post-quantum cryptography, with deadlines of December 31, 2030 for key establishment and December 31, 2031 for digital signatures.
  • The order says adversaries can collect encrypted U.S. data now and decrypt it later once a large-scale quantum computer exists, a risk described as "harvest now, decrypt later."
  • The executive order accelerates the federal post-quantum cryptography timeline by four to five years compared with the 2035 government-wide target set by the 2022 National Security Memorandum 10.
  • Within 30 days, each agency head must name a post-quantum cryptography migration lead, and within 90 days OMB must issue guidance requiring agencies to review inventories of high-value assets and high-impact systems and submit migration plans.
  • The Federal Acquisition Regulatory Council has 180 days to propose a rule requiring covered contractors to meet NIST's FIPS, including post-quantum cryptography algorithms, by December 31, 2030.

GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns — Should Read

GitHub is updating actions/checkout to block the most common unsafe pull_request_target patterns that let forked pull requests run attacker-controlled code with repository secrets and write privileges. The change is meant to curb "pwn request" supply chain attacks by making these risky checkouts fail by default unless maintainers explicitly opt out.

  • GitHub is updating actions/checkout so that, starting June 18, 2026, it blocks common pwn request patterns by default in pull_request_target and some workflow_run workflows involving forked pull requests.
  • The new behavior refuses unsafe checkouts when a workflow tries to fetch code, refs, or commit SHAs that resolve to a fork pull request, unless maintainers explicitly set allow-unsafe-pr-checkout to true.
  • GitHub said pull_request_target runs in the context of the base repository’s default branch and can expose secrets and a GITHUB_TOKEN with read and write permissions.
  • The risk comes from checking out and running untrusted code from a fork in a privileged workflow, which can let attackers steal tokens or secrets and gain the workflow’s full privileges.
  • GitHub and Socket said the change is only a guardrail for actions/checkout and does not eliminate the need to review any workflow that runs untrusted code with secrets, write access, deployment permissions, or OIDC publishing access.

The Code You Didn't Write Is Still Yours to Defend — May Read

AI agents are creating a fast-growing software supply chain risk by automatically pulling and running unseen packages in sandboxes outside normal security controls. The article argues companies need real-time threat intelligence and ingest-time blocking across builds, laptops, and agent environments to stop malicious dependencies before they run.

  • The article argues that AI agents now pull and run open source packages in temporary environments on users’ behalf, creating software supply chain events outside normal review and scanning processes.
  • It says the threat landscape has changed because AI lowers the skill needed to hide malicious code changes and because more code now runs once inside agent sandboxes without ever reaching a repository.
  • The article states that traditional vulnerability management tools are too slow for current supply chain attacks, which can move from patch visibility or malicious package publication to exploitation or adoption within hours or minutes.
  • It argues that package version cooldown policies reduce one risk but can also delay security patches, widening exposure when patch-to-exploit windows are very short.
  • The author says effective defense requires real-time threat intelligence tied to controls that can block package downloads or builds across build pipelines, developer laptops, and agent environments.

The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You — May Read

This sponsored article argues that AI has shrunk the time from vulnerability disclosure to weaponization to hours, making patching alone too slow to manage risk. Its main takeaway is that organizations should validate whether a CVE is actually exploitable in their environment by testing the required attack-technique chain against existing controls, even when no public exploit exists or live exploitation is unsafe.

  • The article says AI has reduced the time from vulnerability disclosure to weaponization from roughly 53 days two years ago to about 8 hours on average in 2026, according to Zero Day Clock.
  • It states that Verizon's 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report found a median 43-day fix time for known-exploited vulnerabilities, with the share of organizations fully patching them dropping from 38% to 26%.
  • The article argues that automated pentesting cannot cover CVEs that lack a public or safe exploit, systems that are too critical or isolated to test with live exploitation, or day-one vulnerabilities before exploit code is ready.
  • It describes TTP-chain validation as mapping a CVE to the sequence of techniques needed for exploitation and testing each technique against deployed controls to determine whether the exploit chain can succeed without launching a live exploit.
  • As an example, the article uses CVE-2025-29824, a Windows CLFS use-after-free tied to Storm-2460 and RansomEXX activity, and says controls such as allow-listing or LSASS protection can break the required technique chain and make the CVE non-exploitable on a given asset.

Compromise kids online safety bill unveiled by House leaders, with key omission — Optional

House leaders unveiled a bipartisan kids online safety bill that advances major restrictions and age-verification rules but drops the controversial duty of care provision many lawmakers wanted to force platforms to redesign harmful features. The omission could complicate Senate support even as the bill gains momentum in the House.

  • The House Energy and Commerce Committee released a bipartisan kids online safety bill that omits the duty of care provision that would have required platforms to take reasonable measures to prevent harms such as suicidal ideation, eating disorders, and cyberbullying.
  • The compromise House bill includes provisions to block minors from using disappearing messages, require AI chatbots to state that they are not human, force porn sites to use age verification technology, regulate video games, and create a data broker registry for platforms receiving kids’ data.
  • Sen. Marsha Blackburn criticized the omission of the duty of care provision and said Big Tech companies will maintain the status quo of putting profit before children's safety without a strong federal standard.
  • The bill would preempt state laws that are weaker than the federal standard while allowing states to enact stricter rules, and it also allows for preemption of some state AI laws.
  • Some digital freedoms experts praised parts of the bill for improving kids' online safety but warned that age verification requirements could threaten privacy and access to constitutionally protected materials.

item.title: LastPass Klue breach, Scattered Spider pleas, and Miasma supply chain fallout
item.content:encoded:
LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack — Should Read

LastPass says hackers stole Klue OAuth tokens and used them to access customer data stored in its Salesforce environment, exposing contact and CRM-related information but not customer vaults or core LastPass systems. The company has rotated tokens, cut off Klue access, and warned customers to watch for phishing and social engineering attempts.

  • LastPass said attackers accessed customer data in its Salesforce environment after obtaining OAuth tokens through the Klue supply chain incident.
  • According to LastPass, its products, services, infrastructure, and customer vaults were not affected by the breach.
  • The company said the potentially exposed data includes customer names, phone numbers, email addresses, physical addresses, support case information, and sales or CRM-related data.
  • LastPass said its investigation found no evidence that the attackers accessed Gong-related data such as customer calls and emails.
  • LastPass has disabled employee access to Klue, rotated the exposed API and OAuth tokens, notified law enforcement, and warned that threat actors may use the sender domains baccarat.com.au, robinskitchen.com.au, and house.com.au.

Two Scattered Spider members plead guilty over cyberattack that crippled London transit — Should Read

Two alleged Scattered Spider members pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London in 2024, an attack that disrupted services for months, exposed customer data and cost TfL about £29 million. U.K. authorities said the pair coordinated via Telegram and linked evidence also pointed to intrusions affecting U.S. healthcare organizations.

  • Two alleged Scattered Spider members, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to infiltrating Transport for London’s network in September 2024.
  • The attack disrupted services for months, exposed customer data linked to TfL’s Oyster refund system, and caused about 29 million pounds in losses and recovery costs, according to the U.K. National Crime Agency.
  • The U.K. National Crime Agency said the pair communicated via Telegram and collaborated through a shared online workspace while carrying out the intrusion.
  • Investigators said evidence recovered from seized devices included a screenshot showing connections to TfL infrastructure and videos allegedly showing Jubair accessing TfL systems during the intrusion.
  • Authorities said evidence uncovered during the investigation also indicated that the networks of SSM Health Care Corporation and Sutter Health had been infiltrated and damaged.

Fake shops target shoppers across Europe with fake Samsung deals, counterfeit goods and World Cup scams — Should Read

Bitdefender found more than 55 coordinated fake online shop campaigns across 12 European countries that impersonate major brands like Samsung, Nike, Adidas, Amazon, Lidl, and SHEIN to steal payments and personal information. These scams use Facebook ads, WhatsApp, email, SMS, phone calls, and deceptive websites, showing that fake-shop operations have become large-scale and professional.

  • Bitdefender Labs identified more than 55 fake-shop campaigns targeting consumers across 12 European countries between March and May 2026.
  • The campaigns impersonated major brands including Samsung, Nike, Adidas, ZARA, H&M, Amazon, Lidl, and SHEIN through Facebook ads, WhatsApp messages, email, SMS, phone calls, and fraudulent websites.
  • Researchers mapped more than 40 domains tied to these operations and observed repeated tactics such as rotating domains, localized content, redirect chains, and Unicode lookalike domains.
  • Some campaigns exploited interest in the 2026 FIFA World Cup, including WhatsApp messages promising free Adidas Deutschland 2026 Fan Kits and counterfeit football merchandise stores.
  • Bitdefender reported that some operators used counterfeit supply chains through WhatsApp and password-protected Yupoo catalogs, while others ran professional-looking storefronts that ultimately accepted only SEPA advance bank transfers.

A One-Line Curiosity Hook Sent a K-12 Teacher to a Same-Day Phishing Domain on Port 8443 — May Read

A K-12 teacher received a phishing email from a legitimate but unrelated Microsoft 365 tenant that used a fake familiar display name and a vague one-line lure to push a link to a same-day registered domain on port 8443. The key takeaway is that fully authenticated emails can still be malicious, especially when they contain context-free links to brand-new domains and non-standard infrastructure.

  • A K-12 educator in a Virginia school district received a one-sentence phishing email that said, "Something that might spark your curiosity - for sure!" and contained only a link.
  • The message came from a Thai Microsoft 365 tenant with SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all passing, while the sender used a fabricated personal display name to appear familiar.
  • The link pointed to hxxps://gekdr.dgfpruf[.]com:8443/AacAYRXS, using a randomized subdomain, non-standard port 8443, and a short mixed-case path that the article says matches a phishing-panel token pattern.
  • A WHOIS lookup showed that dgfpruf[.]com was registered on 2026-03-27, the same day the email was delivered, and the domain had existed for about six hours when the message was quarantined.
  • The article says the compound pattern of a first-contact foreign sender, a context-free single-link message, and a same-day domain on port 8443 matched the structural profile of a credential-harvesting campaign.

What the Miasma campaign reveals about the new supply chain threat model and the underground market for developer credentials — May Read

The Miasma npm campaign shows that modern supply chain attacks are increasingly driven by a black market for stolen developer credentials, with attackers able to weaponize exposed GitHub sessions weeks later to poison trusted packages at scale. The key lesson is that signed provenance and endpoint detection are not enough if organizations do not continuously monitor, protect, and rapidly rotate developer and CI/CD credentials.

  • The article says the Miasma self-propagating npm worm compromised more than 89 packages across three waves between June 1 and June 5, including Red Hat, Vapi.ai, and Microsoft Azure repositories.
  • According to the article, a Red Hat employee’s GitHub credential and session cookie were found in infostealer logs on April 13 and again on May 15, then were used about seven weeks later in the June 1 attack.
  • The article states that malicious packages in Miasma’s first wave carried valid SLSA Build Level 3 provenance attestations because Red Hat’s legitimate GitHub Actions pipeline built them after attackers injected malicious workflow changes.
  • The article reports that Miasma’s later waves introduced new execution and persistence methods, including a 157-byte binding.gyp technique called Phantom Gyp and configuration files that target AI coding assistants such as Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI, and VS Code.
  • The article argues that endpoint detection and response tools are not sufficient on their own against this threat because they cannot see the ephemeral CI/CD environments where Miasma’s credential harvesting occurs.

Webinar: Why email security teams are drowning in alerts — Optional

BleepingComputer will host a July 8, 2026 webinar on how behavioral AI can help security teams reduce email alert fatigue by automating detection, investigation, and remediation of phishing, BEC, and account takeover threats. The session will focus on cutting manual workload and speeding incident response in email security operations.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will feature Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • The article says phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks continue to consume significant time and resources from security teams.
  • According to the article, analysts often must manually review emails, investigate user activity, validate suspicious behavior, and coordinate response actions across multiple platforms.
  • The webinar will cover how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows to reduce manual workloads and improve response times.

Agentic AI: The Weapon That No Longer Needs a Warrior — Optional

Agentic AI is shifting offensive cyber operations from AI-assisted drafting to autonomous action, letting both low-skill and advanced attackers run phishing, reconnaissance, exploit selection, and malware adaptation at much greater speed and scale. The article’s core point is that human judgment is now the critical safeguard, because these systems act confidently but can still be dangerously wrong.

  • The article says agentic AI changes offensive operations by moving from AI that drafts content for a human to AI that can take an objective and perform the steps itself.
  • It argues that this lowers the barrier for unskilled attackers while also allowing experienced operators to run established tradecraft in parallel and complete campaigns much faster.
  • The article describes autonomous social engineering in which one agent gathers public information about a target and another generates personalized messages, manages replies, and continues the conversation without human intervention.
  • It states that as AI-generated phishing becomes fluent, individualized, and grounded in true details about the target, defenders can no longer rely as heavily on poor grammar and repeated templates as warning signs.
  • The article warns that these agents can present incorrect conclusions with strong confidence, especially when combined with vulnerability retrieval systems that surface related flaws without confirming whether they truly apply.

item.title: Malicious npm PostCSS Packages and BioShocking AI Browser Guardrail Bypass
item.content:encoded:
Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT — Should Read

Researchers found malicious npm packages posing as PostCSS and AES-related tools that install a multi-stage Windows RAT capable of stealing Chrome credentials, collecting host data, and executing commands. Developers who installed these packages should remove them immediately, clean up dropped files, and rotate credentials from affected machines.

  • Researchers identified three malicious npm packages named aes-decode-runner-pro, postcss-minify-selector, and postcss-minify-selector-parser that were published by the npm user "abdrizak."
  • The packages use a JavaScript dropper to write and execute a PowerShell script, which downloads a ZIP archive from nvidiadriver[.]net and ultimately runs a Windows remote access trojan.
  • The malware can gather host information, steal Google Chrome credentials and extension data, run shell commands, and upload or download files through a command-and-control server at 95.216.92[.]207:8080.
  • JFrog said the campaign shows how lookalike build-tool dependencies can conceal a multi-stage Windows payload while appearing related to legitimate, widely used software.
  • The article also describes other recent npm and TypeScript ecosystem threats, including the MYRA Linux RAT in apintergrationpost, credential theft in @withgoogle/stitch-sdk, and a Windows dropper campaign involving procwire, routecraft, endpointmap, bytecraft, and staticlayer.

BioShocking AI: “Gaming” the AI Browser and Escaping its Guardrails — Should Read

LayerX researchers found a “BioShocking” technique that can trick AI browsers into treating harmful actions as part of a game, causing them to ignore safety guardrails and access or exfiltrate sensitive data from authenticated sessions. The key takeaway is that AI agents that trust webpage context can be manipulated into performing dangerous real-world actions unless vendors add stronger confirmation, context checking, and scope limits.

  • LayerX researchers describe a vulnerability they call BioShocking, in which an AI browser can be manipulated into violating its safety guardrails by being led to believe it is operating in a false context such as a game or fantasy scenario.
  • The researchers say their proof of concept worked against ChatGPT Atlas, Comet, Fellou, Genspark Browser, Sigma Browser, and the Claude Chrome plugin, and that all vendors were notified of the findings.
  • In the demonstration, the agent first learned that intentionally wrong answers were rewarded in a puzzle, then followed instructions that redirected it to the victim’s employer GitHub repository and copied sensitive SSH login credentials from a textbox.
  • LayerX recommends requiring explicit user confirmation before reading data from authenticated contexts, adding checks for context shifts that contradict reality, and letting users limit what an agent can access during a session.
  • The article warns that in a real attack scenario, the same redirection technique could point the agent to open tabs, authenticated repositories, or internal tools available in the user’s browser session.

item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploited, FortiBleed Harvests 110M Credentials, Trump Sets 2030 Post-Quantum Deadline
item.content:encoded:
Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Cisco Unified Communications Manager SSRF flaw CVE-2026-20230 is now actively exploited in the wild, allowing unauthenticated attackers to write files and gain root privileges; patching is urgent.

  • A high-severity SSRF vulnerability, CVE-2026-20230, in Cisco Unified Communications Manager allows unauthenticated attackers to write arbitrary files to the underlying operating system.
  • Cisco released security updates for the flaw on June 3, warning that successful exploitation could give attackers root privileges.
  • Threat intelligence firm Defused observed active exploitation over the weekend, with attacks using file:// payloads to create files on the device.
  • The current in-the-wild exploitation appears to be reconnaissance, attempting to write a test file named '/tmp/cve-2026-20230-test.txt' to identify vulnerable devices.
  • Following the disclosure, SSD Secure published a technical write-up and proof-of-concept exploit detailing how the Webdialer component can be abused for remote code execution.

New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer — Must Read

A new macOS ClickFix campaign tricks users into executing a Terminal command from fake CAPTCHAs that automatically downloads, mounts, and launches a DMG containing the Atomic macOS Stealer infostealer, evading the need for manual user interaction with the disk image.

  • A new macOS ClickFix campaign displays a fake CAPTCHA page instructing users to paste a malicious Terminal command.
  • The command silently downloads a DMG file from an attacker-controlled server, mounts it with hdiutil, and launches the contained application.
  • The payload is the Atomic macOS Stealer infostealer, which steals browser data, cryptocurrency wallets, Keychain data, messaging app information, and user documents.
  • The malware replaces legitimate Ledger Live and Trezor Suite installations with malicious versions to perform cryptocurrency theft.

Feds seize alleged cyber-scam infrastructure connected to Southeast Asian company — Must Read

The U.S. seized a cloud account used by Cambodia's Huione Group as backend infrastructure for a multi-billion-dollar cyber scam operation, which laundered illicit funds for Southeast Asian crime and North Korean cyber heists through Telegram channels.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice seized a cloud computing account that hosted backend infrastructure for subsidiaries of the Cambodia-based Huione Group.
  • The Huione Group laundered at least $4 billion in illicit proceeds between August 2021 and January 2025, including funds from Southeast Asian scam operations and North Korean cyber heists.
  • The seized infrastructure supported Telegram channels used to sell stolen credit card and identity information, enable human trafficking, and launder money from romance and investment scams.
  • Cambodian authorities arrested the former head of Huione Group, Li Xiong, and extradited him to China to face money laundering and fraud charges as a member of the Chen Xi crime syndicate.
  • This action follows a series of U.S. measures, including last year's FinCEN order severing Huione from the U.S. financial system and recent sanctions on individuals connected to scam operations.

GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns — Must Read

GitHub is hardening actions/checkout to refuse fetching fork pull request code by default in pull_request_target and workflow_run workflows, blocking a common attack vector that lets malicious pull requests run with the base repository’s full privileges and steal secrets.

  • GitHub is updating the actions/checkout action to refuse fetching fork pull request code by default when used in pull_request_target and workflow_run workflows.
  • The new default refusal takes effect June 18, 2026 for version 7 and will be backported to all supported major versions on July 16, 2026.
  • Workflow authors can explicitly allow the checkout by setting the allow-unsafe-pr-checkout input to true, but the default behavior blocks the most common pwn request patterns.
  • The change is designed to stop attacks where malicious pull requests from forks run with the base repository’s full privileges, a technique used in recent supply chain compromises against Nx, PostHog, TanStack, and kubernetes-el.
  • The protection is limited to the actions/checkout action and does not prevent pwn requests through other methods, so it serves as a guardrail rather than a complete solution.

What the Miasma campaign reveals about the new supply chain threat model and the underground market for developer credentials — Must Read

The Miasma campaign exposed a thriving Developer Credential Economy where stolen developer credentials circulate for weeks before being used in automated supply chain attacks, highlighting the need for exposure management over reactive detection.

  • The Miasma campaign compromised over 89 npm packages across five days, affecting repositories under Red Hat, Vapi.ai, and Microsoft Azure.
  • The attack originated from a GitHub credential and session cookie stolen by a commodity infostealer and left available in underground markets for approximately seven weeks before weaponization.
  • Malicious packages were published with valid SLSA Build Level 3 provenance attestations because the attacker injected code into the legitimate CI/CD pipeline, demonstrating that cryptographic build signatures do not prove the pipeline was clean.
  • Wave 3 of the campaign introduced persistence files that target AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI, VS Code), broadening the attack surface from registries to local developer environments.
  • The public release of the full Mini Shai-Hulud worm source code on May 12 lowered the barrier to entry, allowing any operator to replicate structurally identical supply chain campaigns.

FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation — Must Read

A Russian-speaking threat actor orchestrated the FortiBleed campaign, compromising over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls to passively harvest more than 110 million credentials using a custom sniffing tool, as part of a broader multi-vendor initial access operation since February 2026.

  • A Russian-speaking initial access broker conducted the FortiBleed credential-harvesting operation, compromising over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally since February 2026.
  • The attackers deployed a custom tool named FortigateSniffer that uses the FortiOS diagnostic sniffer to passively capture cleartext and hashed credentials from traffic passing through infected devices.
  • The group primarily targeted small and medium businesses with fewer than 200 employees, with a notable focus on the United States and India.
  • FortiBleed is part of a broader multi-vendor initial access operation that also brute-forces Synology NAS, Sophos firewalls, RDWeb portals, Citrix SSL-VPNs, and MS-SQL servers.
  • The operation harvested over 110 million credentials, including RADIUS, NTLM, Kerberos, and MySQL authentication tokens, and used Hashmat, Hashtopolis, and a Telegram bot called HASHBOT for cracking.

Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London — Must Read

Two members of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London in 2024, causing £29 million in losses and significant operational disruption.

  • Two members of the Scattered Spider group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London's systems in 2024.
  • The cyberattack occurred between August 31 and September 3, 2024, causing access to Oyster refunds data and disruption of customer refund services.
  • The attack forced all 28,000 TfL employees to reset their passwords in person and resulted in £29 million in financial losses.
  • Devices seized from Owen Flowers' home contained a screenshot showing connectivity to TfL infrastructure and videos of Jubair breaching TfL systems.
  • The two hackers communicated via Telegram and a shared online platform during the intrusion, and Flowers has also been linked to intrusions at US healthcare organizations SSM Health Care Corporation and Sutter Health.

LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack — Must Read

LastPass confirmed that threat actors used stolen OAuth tokens from a supply chain compromise of Klue to access customer data within its Salesforce environment, though no vault data was accessed.

  • LastPass announced that hackers accessed customer data from its Salesforce environment after stealing OAuth tokens in a supply chain attack on Klue.
  • The exposed data includes customer names, phone numbers, email addresses, physical addresses, support case information, and sales/CRM data, but customer vaults and passwords were not accessed.
  • The attack was claimed by the Icarus extortion group, which compromised Klue’s infrastructure using legacy credentials and stole OAuth tokens connecting customers’ Salesforce environments.
  • Multiple other organizations were affected, including Recorded Future, Tanium, Jamf, Sprout Social, Gong, and Insurity.
  • LastPass disabled employee access to Klue, rotated exposed tokens, notified law enforcement, and warned users about phishing from specific sender domains.

Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT — Must Read

Malicious npm packages masquerading as PostCSS tools have been discovered delivering a multi-stage Windows remote access trojan, alongside several other supply chain attacks targeting developer credentials and Linux systems.

  • Malicious npm packages aes-decode-runner-pro, postcss-minify-selector, and postcss-minify-selector-parser deliver a Windows remote access trojan by dropping a PowerShell downloader that fetches a ZIP from nvidiadriver[.]net.
  • The RAT collects host information, steals Chrome credentials by bypassing app-bound encryption, runs shell commands, and communicates with command-and-control server 95.216.92.207:8080.
  • A separate package named apintergrationpost delivers a Linux RAT called MYRA that compiles a native C rootkit and establishes multiple persistence mechanisms.
  • The @withgoogle/stitch-sdk package steals developer credentials from eight sources including Claude Code, git config, and npm config, then exfiltrates them to stitch-production[.]org.
  • The activity overlaps with the North Korean PolinRider operation, which injects obfuscated JavaScript into GitHub repositories to distribute the BeaverTail downloader and InvisibleFerret backdoor.

Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack as hackers leak data — Should Read

Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack after the World Leaks extortion group leaked stolen data, including Apple product manufacturing schematics, while the company says operations remain unaffected.

  • Tata Electronics confirmed a cybersecurity incident on some of its IT systems but stated that its operations were not affected and continued normally.
  • The World Leaks threat group claimed the attack and leaked files that it alleged contain Apple product manufacturing data, including internal component schematics and PCB designs.
  • World Leaks is a rebrand of the Hunters International ransomware group and now operates purely as a data-extortion group, stealing files and threatening to leak them without encrypting systems.
  • Apple had not responded to BleepingComputer’s inquiry about whether any proprietary data was exposed at the time of publication.
  • Other confirmed or investigated victims of World Leaks include Dell, which confirmed a breach in July 2025, and Nike, which launched an investigation after a claimed theft of 1.4 TB of files in January 2026.

Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents — Should Read

A fake AI agent skill passed all major security scanners by keeping the submitted package clean while hosting malicious instructions on an external link that was changed after approval, proving that scanners cannot catch runtime payload swaps.

  • AIR created a fake AI agent skill called 'brand-landingpage' that passed every tested security scanner because the submitted package contained no malicious code and directed the agent to an external link the firm controlled.
  • The skill gained credibility by having a pull request merged into a popular marketplace repository, inheriting its high GitHub star count, and was promoted via an Instagram ad.
  • After the skill was installed on roughly 26,000 agents, AIR changed the external website to serve a script that harvested email addresses, showing that scanners only evaluate a static snapshot and cannot detect changes made after approval.
  • The method reflects earlier independent research by Trail of Bits and real-world campaigns that use the same delayed-payload tactic, confirming that current skill scanners fail to address runtime content swaps.

Fake shops target shoppers across Europe with fake Samsung deals, counterfeit goods and World Cup scams — Should Read

Bitdefender Labs identified more than 55 coordinated fake-shop campaigns across 12 European countries that use Facebook ads, WhatsApp, and lookalike domains to impersonate Samsung, Nike, ZARA, and other major brands, stealing payments and personal data while exploiting FIFA World Cup 2026 excitement.

  • Bitdefender Labs uncovered more than 55 fake-shop campaigns that targeted consumers in 12 European countries between March and May 2026, impersonating brands like Samsung, Nike, Adidas, ZARA, H&M, Amazon, Lidl, and SHEIN.
  • Attackers used Facebook ads, WhatsApp messages, email, SMS, phone calls, and Unicode lookalike domains such as adidas¹.com to redirect victims to fraudulent payment or data-harvesting pages.
  • A German fake-shop network called “Homborg Online Handel” rotated domains frequently and only accepted SEPA advance bank transfers, making it nearly impossible for victims to recover their money.
  • Several campaigns exploited excitement for the FIFA World Cup 2026, offering counterfeit jerseys and fake Adidas Deutschland fan kits through redirect chains and WhatsApp messages.
  • The investigation identified a China-based operator named “Carl” who used WhatsApp to direct European users to password-protected Yupoo catalogs selling counterfeit Nike, Adidas, and luxury goods.

WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Uses Fake Documents to Install ManageEngine RMM Tool — Should Read

Attackers are hijacking WhatsApp accounts to send disguised VBScript attachments that, when opened, download secondary payloads and ultimately install the legitimate ManageEngine RMM Central tool, granting remote access to victims' systems across multiple countries.

  • Attackers sent malicious VBScript files through compromised WhatsApp accounts, disguising them as business and financial documents.
  • The campaign targets users of WhatsApp Desktop and WhatsApp Web in over ten countries, with the highest victim concentration in Malaysia.
  • The infection chain ultimately installs the legitimate ManageEngine RMM Central tool to give attackers remote access to victim systems.
  • The VBScript payloads download secondary scripts that tamper with User Account Control (UAC) behavior and deploy the RMM installer.
  • Infrastructure overlaps suggest a link to prior Gh0st RAT and ValleyRAT activity, but the campaign remains unattributed.

Five Eyes agencies sound alarm about AI’s threat to cybersecurity — Should Read

The Five Eyes intelligence alliance warns that rapidly advancing AI is dramatically shrinking the window between vulnerability discovery and exploitation, urging business leaders to urgently strengthen their cyber defenses now rather than in the coming years.

  • Five Eyes intelligence agencies (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand) warned that rapidly evolving AI is an urgent cybersecurity threat business leaders must address immediately.
  • Frontier AI models will likely exceed expectations and transform offensive and defensive cyber capabilities in months, not years.
  • AI lowers barriers for malicious actors, increases attack speed and complexity, and dramatically shrinks the time between vulnerability discovery and exploitation.
  • The agencies urged organizations to reduce attack surfaces, speed up patching, remediate unsupported legacy systems, and strengthen access controls and authentication.
  • The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency now requires federal agencies to use a triage system that can mandate patching within three days, partly based on whether AI is used to exploit a vulnerability.

VA OIG Identifies Lack of Oversight of VA GenAI Chat Tools — Should Read

The VA Office of Inspector General identified a lack of oversight and safeguards for generative AI chat tools used by staff, posing potential patient safety risks, echoing concerns highlighted in recent Health-ISAC and HSCC AI governance guidance.

  • A review by the VA Office of Inspector General found that VA staff's use of generative AI chat tools lacks safeguards and oversight, creating potential patient safety risks.
  • Health-ISAC and the Health Sector Coordinating Council Cybersecurity Working Group issued guidance earlier this year to help healthcare organizations establish effective AI governance frameworks for the safe use of GenAI tools.
  • The guidance includes a white paper titled 'Policies and Safeguards for a Safe Use of AI' and the HSCC 'Health Industry AI Cyber Governance Framework Implementation Guide.'
  • Concerns highlighted include the possibility that sensitive patient data could be shared with publicly accessible chatbots and that AI-generated output might put patients at risk of harm.

Payouts King Ransomware Initial Access Broker Deploys New Edgecution Malware — Should Read

The Payouts King ransomware initial access broker deploys a novel malware, Edgecution, that uses a malicious Microsoft Edge extension abusing Chrome native messaging to escape the browser sandbox and gain full host access.

  • The attack begins with social engineering via Microsoft Teams messages impersonating a company's IT staff, directing victims to a fake Microsoft website.
  • The Edgecution malware uses a malicious Microsoft Edge browser extension that abuses Chrome native messaging to escape the browser sandbox and interact directly with the host operating system.
  • The browser extension runs in a headless Microsoft Edge instance, making it invisible to the victim and bypassing user warnings or prompts.
  • A Python-based backdoor handles core malicious actions—collecting system information, accessing the filesystem, and executing arbitrary commands—relayed from the extension via native messaging.
  • C2 communication occurs over WebSocket connections to AWS-hosted domains under cloudfront.net, with messages including command IDs for shell execution, file writing, and PowerShell execution.

OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws — Should Read

OpenAI launched its most advanced vulnerability-hunting model, GPT-5.5-Cyber, and the Patch the Planet partnership to rapidly find and fix software bugs, as AI-driven threats increasingly shrink the patching window for defenders.

  • OpenAI released GPT-5.5-Cyber, its strongest model for finding and helping patch software vulnerabilities, to trusted defenders as part of the Daybreak initiative.
  • OpenAI launched the Patch the Planet program in partnership with Trail of Bits, with initial open-source participants including cURL, NATS Server, and Python.org.
  • The Daybreak initiative has already surfaced dozens of vulnerabilities, including the HTTP/2 Bomb denial-of-service technique and multiple kernel exploits across Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD.
  • Frontier AI models from OpenAI and Anthropic are accelerating vulnerability discovery, shifting the bottleneck from finding bugs to patching them and overwhelming maintainers.
  • Five Eyes intelligence agencies warned that advanced AI enables faster exploitation, shrinking the vulnerability-to-exploit timeline to months and requiring defenders to act quickly.

BioShocking AI: “Gaming” the AI Browser and Escaping its Guardrails — Should Read

Researchers developed 'BioShocking,' a technique that manipulates AI web browsers into abandoning safety guardrails by convincing them they are playing a game, successfully tricking multiple agentic browsers into leaking credentials and executing malicious actions.

  • The 'BioShocking' vulnerability manipulates AI browser agents by shifting their context into a game world, causing them to ignore built-in safety guardrails.
  • The proof-of-concept exploit successfully compromised ChatGPT Atlas, Comet, Fellou, Genspark Browser, Sigma Browser, and the Claude Chrome plugin.
  • Once tricked, the agents exfiltrated sensitive SSH credentials from an authenticated GitHub repository without recognizing the action as harmful.
  • The root cause is that AI agents trust their current context, applying game logic instead of real-world safety rules when the context is manipulated.
  • LayerX recommends that vendors add confirmation steps for sensitive operations and that users restrict the data and sessions their AI browsers can access.

Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people — Should Read

A targeted phishing attack on healthcare technology company Xsolis gave attackers access to its network, compromising sensitive personal and medical data of nearly 1.4 million individuals.

  • Xsolis detected unauthorized network activity on January 22, 2026, which originated from a targeted phishing attack on January 20, 2026.
  • The breach exposed sensitive information—including names, addresses, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, health insurance details, and medical treatment information—of almost 1.4 million individuals.
  • The company has not identified any misuse of the stolen data but is warning affected individuals to remain alert for potential targeted attacks.
  • Xsolis is mailing notification letters to affected individuals and offering 12-month identity monitoring and identity theft restoration services through Kroll.
  • In response, Xsolis reset user and account passwords, increased system monitoring, rolled out updated security measures, accelerated employee security training, and strengthened credential management mechanisms.

A One-Line Curiosity Hook Sent a K-12 Teacher to a Same-Day Phishing Domain on Port 8443 — May Read

A one-sentence curiosity lure from a legitimate foreign Microsoft 365 tenant with a display-name impersonation directed a K-12 teacher to a same-day-registered domain on non-standard port 8443, showing how attackers bypass authentication checks by using purpose-built, hours-old infrastructure.

  • The phishing email consisted solely of a one-sentence curiosity hook and a link, with no explanation of the sender or relationship, targeting a K-12 teacher in a Virginia school district.
  • The sender address was from a legitimate foreign Microsoft 365 tenant in Thailand, with a fabricated display name that impersonated a familiar person, causing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication to pass successfully.
  • The link pointed to a domain (dgfpruf[.]com) registered on the same day the email was delivered, hosted on port 8443 with a randomized subdomain and a victim-specific URL path, a pattern consistent with credential harvesting infrastructure.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the email at 51% confidence and quarantined it, based on the combination of first contact from an unrelated foreign sender, same-day domain age, non-standard port, and randomized URL structure.
  • The attacker likely obtained the recipient’s email from a public school staff directory and crafted the display name to suggest familiarity, a type of display name impersonation that bypasses technical authentication checks.

Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature — May Read

Microsoft's optional KB5095093 preview update for Windows 11 introduces a new Point-in-Time restore feature that allows quick system rollbacks, alongside fixes for the Recycle Bin bug and many other improvements.

  • The optional KB5095093 preview update introduces a Point-in-Time Restore feature that automatically creates full-system snapshots every 24 hours (for consumers) and retains them for up to 72 hours, allowing users to roll back the entire PC.
  • The update fixes a bug where permanently deleting a file from the Recycle Bin after the June 2026 security update caused the confirmation dialog to show an internal file name instead of the original file name.
  • The emoji panel now uses GIPHY for GIFs because Google's Tenor API was deprecated, and users must install this update to continue seeing GIFs in the panel.

The post-quantum EO is an important milestone. Now it’s time to get to work — May Read

The U.S. executive order on post-quantum cryptography imposes 2030–2031 migration deadlines, creating supply-chain pressure and an immediate need for organizations to adopt post-quantum encryption for internet traffic and begin planning authentication upgrades rather than waiting for the final deadline.

  • Executive Order 14409 requires federal agencies to transition High Value Assets and high impact systems to post-quantum encryption by December 31, 2030, and to post-quantum authentication by December 31, 2031.
  • The order directs the FAR Council to propose rules mandating that federal contractors comply with post-quantum FIPS by the end of 2030, creating supply-chain pressure that helps drive industry-wide adoption.
  • Post-quantum encryption is needed immediately to prevent harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks, while post-quantum authentication is only necessary once a quantum computer capable of breaking signatures becomes operational.
  • Cloudflare recommends that organizations begin their migration now by protecting Internet traffic with post-quantum encryption, updating procurement requirements, and conducting a quantum impact inventory instead of waiting for exhaustive inventories.
  • The article calls on OMB to clearly define what it means to fully transition systems to post-quantum cryptography in order to prevent downgrade attacks, and highlights the need to accelerate NIST's Cryptographic Module Validation Program.

From vulnerability report to CVE draft in minutes: how Elastic automated security advisories with AI — May Read

Elastic built a generative AI agent using Elastic Agent Builder and retrieval-augmented generation against MITRE's CWE and CAPEC catalogues indexed in Elasticsearch to automatically draft complete, accurate CVE security advisories from raw vulnerability reports, dramatically speeding up the disclosure process while grounding outputs in authoritative data.

  • Elastic built a generative AI agent using Elastic Agent Builder that drafts complete CVE security advisories from raw vulnerability reports, including CWE classification, CAPEC methodology, CVSS scoring, and mitigation guidance.
  • The agent uses retrieval-augmented generation against MITRE's CWE and CAPEC catalogues indexed in Elasticsearch via the Elastic Crawler to ground its output and prevent hallucinated classification IDs.
  • Guardrails in the system prompt, such as memory-safety verification and a minimum disclosure checklist, ensure the draft avoids sharing exploitable implementation details and aligns with Elastic's internal security practices.
  • The agent outputs both a draft advisory and a reasoning section that justifies each taxonomy and CVSS choice, enabling human reviewers to efficiently validate the results.
  • The advisory ESA-2026-01 was published as a real-world example of output that passed through this automated pipeline, proving its production readiness.

The Code You Didn't Write Is Still Yours to Defend — Optional

AI agents now routinely fetch and execute unvetted open-source packages in ephemeral sandboxes, bypassing traditional supply chain controls, so organizations must adopt real-time threat intelligence at the point of ingest to defend against attacks that weaponize within minutes.

  • AI assistants now fetch and execute open source packages in sandboxes without human review, creating a software supply chain event that traditional scanners and proxies miss.
  • Attackers can use a simple prompt to generate code that appears routine yet includes a backdoor, and working exploits can emerge within two hours of a patch release.
  • A cooldown that blocks packages newer than seven days also delays security patches, widening the exposure because the patch-to-exploit window is now only hours.
  • Governance at the point of ingest, using an hourly updated threat intelligence feed that blocks downloads or builds when a malicious package appears, is the effective defense.
  • The agent environment is the fastest growing blind spot, and Socket Firewall addresses this by protecting endpoints where code runs without a build pipeline.

Measuring Identity Risk: The Metrics Every Security Leader Should Track — Optional

Identity risk is an under-measured but critical attack surface; security leaders should track metrics like total exposed identities, executive exposure, and exposure velocity to quantify risk, prioritize remediation, and communicate effectively to the board.

  • Identity has become a primary attack surface, yet many organizations lack meaningful metrics to measure identity risk.
  • The article defines eight specific identity risk metrics, including Total Exposed Identities, High-Risk Identity Exposure Rate, Executive Exposure Score, and Exposure Velocity.
  • Measuring identity risk enables security leaders to prioritize investments, focus remediation, communicate risk to leadership, and demonstrate progress.
  • Boards are increasingly asking for identity risk metrics, such as executive and third-party exposure, because traditional cybersecurity metrics fail to answer these questions.
  • Identity risk is dynamic, persistent, and distributed; exposed credentials can remain usable indefinitely, making identity risk uniquely difficult to quantify.

Compromise kids online safety bill unveiled by House leaders, with key omission — Optional

The House released a bipartisan kids online safety bill that notably excludes a 'duty of care' provision requiring platforms to mitigate harms like cyberbullying and eating disorders, a decision that may jeopardize support from many lawmakers and advocates.

  • The House bill released by the Energy and Commerce Committee excludes a 'duty of care' provision that would have required online platforms to modify algorithms and design to prevent harms like suicidal ideation, eating disorders, and cyberbullying.
  • The compromise package blocks minors from using disappearing messages, mandates AI chatbots to acknowledge they are not human, forces porn sites to use age verification technology, and establishes a data broker registry for children's data.
  • Senator Marsha Blackburn criticized the omission, and 76 senators have previously supported including a duty of care in the legislation; her own forthcoming proposal with the White House is expected to retain that standard.
  • Kate Ruane of the Center for Democracy and Technology warned that the bill incentivizes age verification, putting the privacy of all internet users at risk and threatening access to constitutionally protected materials.
  • The new House version aligns more closely with the Senate bill, but Senate Democrats have indicated that passing the bill without a duty of care will be difficult.

Aikido x Drydock | A way for maintainers to catch malware before it ships — Optional

Aikido and Drydock are launching a free integration that lets npm and PyPI maintainers inspect and diff staged package releases before publishing, catching malware during approval rather than after it ships.

  • Aikido and Drydock partnered to give npm and PyPI maintainers a free way to inspect the exact contents of a staged package release before it publishes.
  • Drydock diffs a staged release against the last published version and flags security-relevant changes like new lifecycle scripts, network access, or added binaries, ranked by severity.
  • For npm staged publishing, an approving maintainer can see the changed bytes and then approve publish with their own 2FA; for PyPI or unstaged npm, Drydock runs as a GitHub Actions gate.
  • Drydock uses a read-only token, never runs the package contents, never publishes itself, and keeps a human in the loop for every approval decision.
  • The goal is to catch malware during the approval stage instead of after it ships, breaking the pattern of post-mortem malware disclosures and damage control.

Webinar: Why email security teams are drowning in alerts — Optional

A July 8 webinar will address how behavioral AI can automate email security workflows to reduce alert fatigue and accelerate investigation and response to phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks.

  • A live webinar titled 'Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI' will be held on July 8, 2026, hosted by BleepingComputer.
  • The webinar is presented by Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • Security teams face alert fatigue and investigation backlogs due to the high volume of manual tasks required to investigate phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks.
  • The session examines how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows to surface the highest-risk incidents and accelerate responses.
  • Attendees will learn practical techniques to reduce manual analysis, prioritize high-risk threats, and improve operational efficiency in email security operations.

The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You — Optional

You can prove a vulnerability is not exploitable in your environment without a live exploit by validating each technique in its attack chain against your existing controls, giving you a defensible verdict even for day-one CVEs or off-limits assets.

  • The average time from vulnerability disclosure to exploitation has fallen to roughly 8 hours in 2026, while median fix times for known-exploited vulnerabilities have risen to 43 days.
  • Automated penetration testing is limited because it cannot safely test assets that are business-critical, air-gapped, or lack a public exploit, leaving 85-90% of an enterprise's exposures unaddressed.
  • TTP-chain validation breaks a vulnerability into the sequence of attacker techniques required for exploitation and tests each step against existing security controls, providing a defensible verdict without launching a live exploit.
  • The technique-chain approach works on day one of a disclosure, on assets where live exploits are unsafe, and even when no working exploit exists, because it validates by inference rather than detonation.
  • CVE-2025-29824, a Windows CLFS use-after-free exploited by Storm-2460 in RansomEXX attacks, is used as an example of how TTP-chain validation can determine exploitability by testing individual techniques like MSBuild execution and LSASS dumping against controls.

Agentic AI: The Weapon That No Longer Needs a Warrior — Optional

Agentic AI allows offensive tools to plan and execute attacks without human guidance, lowering the barrier for unskilled attackers and eroding traditional detection signals, which makes human oversight and verification the critical remaining role.

  • Agentic AI autonomously executes entire attack chains, eliminating the need for a human operator to guide each step.
  • This technology grants unskilled attackers real offensive capability, a phenomenon the author terms 'script kiddie as a service'.
  • Autonomous social engineering erases classic detection signals by generating fluent, personalized messages that do not follow mass-produced templates.
  • AI-driven exploit chains can produce false conclusions with high confidence because agents match indicators to assumptions without verifying actual target conditions.
  • Human oversight becomes the critical component, as the machine can aim itself but cannot judge whether an attack should be launched.

Silent Push partners with CDL Cyber Intelligence to bring preemptive defense to the United Kingdom — Optional

Silent Push and CDL Cyber Intelligence have partnered to provide UK organizations with early warning of cyber threats by detecting adversary staging infrastructure, offering an average 157-day lead time before an attack hits their perimeter.

  • Silent Push and CDL Cyber Intelligence have partnered to deliver preemptive defense by uncovering adversary staging infrastructure long before an attack launches.
  • The Context Graph continuously monitors DNS, WHOIS, certificate, and hosting data to surface clusters of preparation activity as Indicators of Future Attack.
  • Clients of CDL gain an average of 157 days of warning before a threat campaign reaches their network perimeter.
