  • The UK is a high-risk target for state-linked threat actors due to its roles as a NATO member, financial hub, and home to critical infrastructure.
  • Traffic Origin technology reveals the true upstream country controlling a connection, even when attackers mask it with local-looking residential proxies or VPNs.

item.title: Executive Order 14409 Mandates Post-Quantum Crypto Migration by 2030; CVE-2026-20230 Exploited and FortiBleed Harvests 110M Credentials
item.content:encoded:
Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks — Must Read

Cisco Unified Communications Manager SSRF flaw CVE-2026-20230 is now actively exploited in the wild, allowing unauthenticated attackers to write files and gain root privileges; patching is urgent.

  • A high-severity SSRF vulnerability, CVE-2026-20230, in Cisco Unified Communications Manager allows unauthenticated attackers to write arbitrary files to the underlying operating system.
  • Cisco released security updates for the flaw on June 3, warning that successful exploitation could give attackers root privileges.
  • Threat intelligence firm Defused observed active exploitation over the weekend, with attacks using file:// payloads to create files on the device.
  • The current in-the-wild exploitation appears to be reconnaissance, attempting to write a test file named '/tmp/cve-2026-20230-test.txt' to identify vulnerable devices.
  • Following the disclosure, SSD Secure published a technical write-up and proof-of-concept exploit detailing how the Webdialer component can be abused for remote code execution.

New macOS ClickFix attack silently mounts DMGs to push infostealer — Must Read

A new macOS ClickFix campaign tricks users into executing a Terminal command from fake CAPTCHAs that automatically downloads, mounts, and launches a DMG containing the Atomic macOS Stealer infostealer, evading the need for manual user interaction with the disk image.

  • A new macOS ClickFix campaign displays a fake CAPTCHA page instructing users to paste a malicious Terminal command.
  • The command silently downloads a DMG file from an attacker-controlled server, mounts it with hdiutil, and launches the contained application.
  • The payload is the Atomic macOS Stealer infostealer, which steals browser data, cryptocurrency wallets, Keychain data, messaging app information, and user documents.
  • The malware replaces legitimate Ledger Live and Trezor Suite installations with malicious versions to perform cryptocurrency theft.

Feds seize alleged cyber-scam infrastructure connected to Southeast Asian company — Must Read

The U.S. seized a cloud account used by Cambodia's Huione Group as backend infrastructure for a multi-billion-dollar cyber scam operation, which laundered illicit funds for Southeast Asian crime and North Korean cyber heists through Telegram channels.

  • The U.S. Department of Justice seized a cloud computing account that hosted backend infrastructure for subsidiaries of the Cambodia-based Huione Group.
  • The Huione Group laundered at least $4 billion in illicit proceeds between August 2021 and January 2025, including funds from Southeast Asian scam operations and North Korean cyber heists.
  • The seized infrastructure supported Telegram channels used to sell stolen credit card and identity information, enable human trafficking, and launder money from romance and investment scams.
  • Cambodian authorities arrested the former head of Huione Group, Li Xiong, and extradited him to China to face money laundering and fraud charges as a member of the Chen Xi crime syndicate.
  • This action follows a series of U.S. measures, including last year's FinCEN order severing Huione from the U.S. financial system and recent sanctions on individuals connected to scam operations.

GitHub Updates actions/checkout to Block Common Pwn Request Attack Patterns — Must Read

GitHub is hardening actions/checkout to refuse fetching fork pull request code by default in pull_request_target and workflow_run workflows, blocking a common attack vector that lets malicious pull requests run with the base repository’s full privileges and steal secrets.

  • GitHub is updating the actions/checkout action to refuse fetching fork pull request code by default when used in pull_request_target and workflow_run workflows.
  • The new default refusal takes effect June 18, 2026 for version 7 and will be backported to all supported major versions on July 16, 2026.
  • Workflow authors can explicitly allow the checkout by setting the allow-unsafe-pr-checkout input to true, but the default behavior blocks the most common pwn request patterns.
  • The change is designed to stop attacks where malicious pull requests from forks run with the base repository’s full privileges, a technique used in recent supply chain compromises against Nx, PostHog, TanStack, and kubernetes-el.
  • The protection is limited to the actions/checkout action and does not prevent pwn requests through other methods, so it serves as a guardrail rather than a complete solution.

What the Miasma campaign reveals about the new supply chain threat model and the underground market for developer credentials — Must Read

The Miasma campaign exposed a thriving Developer Credential Economy where stolen developer credentials circulate for weeks before being used in automated supply chain attacks, highlighting the need for exposure management over reactive detection.

  • The Miasma campaign compromised over 89 npm packages across five days, affecting repositories under Red Hat, Vapi.ai, and Microsoft Azure.
  • The attack originated from a GitHub credential and session cookie stolen by a commodity infostealer and left available in underground markets for approximately seven weeks before weaponization.
  • Malicious packages were published with valid SLSA Build Level 3 provenance attestations because the attacker injected code into the legitimate CI/CD pipeline, demonstrating that cryptographic build signatures do not prove the pipeline was clean.
  • Wave 3 of the campaign introduced persistence files that target AI coding assistants (Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI, VS Code), broadening the attack surface from registries to local developer environments.
  • The public release of the full Mini Shai-Hulud worm source code on May 12 lowered the barrier to entry, allowing any operator to replicate structurally identical supply chain campaigns.

FortiBleed Targeted FortiGate Firewalls in 110 Million-Credential Harvesting Operation — Must Read

A Russian-speaking threat actor orchestrated the FortiBleed campaign, compromising over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls to passively harvest more than 110 million credentials using a custom sniffing tool, as part of a broader multi-vendor initial access operation since February 2026.

  • A Russian-speaking initial access broker conducted the FortiBleed credential-harvesting operation, compromising over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally since February 2026.
  • The attackers deployed a custom tool named FortigateSniffer that uses the FortiOS diagnostic sniffer to passively capture cleartext and hashed credentials from traffic passing through infected devices.
  • The group primarily targeted small and medium businesses with fewer than 200 employees, with a notable focus on the United States and India.
  • FortiBleed is part of a broader multi-vendor initial access operation that also brute-forces Synology NAS, Sophos firewalls, RDWeb portals, Citrix SSL-VPNs, and MS-SQL servers.
  • The operation harvested over 110 million credentials, including RADIUS, NTLM, Kerberos, and MySQL authentication tokens, and used Hashmat, Hashtopolis, and a Telegram bot called HASHBOT for cracking.

Scattered Spider members plead guilty to hacking Transport for London — Must Read

Two members of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London in 2024, causing £29 million in losses and significant operational disruption.

  • Two members of the Scattered Spider group, Thalha Jubair and Owen Flowers, pleaded guilty to hacking Transport for London's systems in 2024.
  • The cyberattack occurred between August 31 and September 3, 2024, causing access to Oyster refunds data and disruption of customer refund services.
  • The attack forced all 28,000 TfL employees to reset their passwords in person and resulted in £29 million in financial losses.
  • Devices seized from Owen Flowers' home contained a screenshot showing connectivity to TfL infrastructure and videos of Jubair breaching TfL systems.
  • The two hackers communicated via Telegram and a shared online platform during the intrusion, and Flowers has also been linked to intrusions at US healthcare organizations SSM Health Care Corporation and Sutter Health.

LastPass confirms data breach in Klue supply chain attack — Must Read

LastPass confirmed that threat actors used stolen OAuth tokens from a supply chain compromise of Klue to access customer data within its Salesforce environment, though no vault data was accessed.

  • LastPass announced that hackers accessed customer data from its Salesforce environment after stealing OAuth tokens in a supply chain attack on Klue.
  • The exposed data includes customer names, phone numbers, email addresses, physical addresses, support case information, and sales/CRM data, but customer vaults and passwords were not accessed.
  • The attack was claimed by the Icarus extortion group, which compromised Klue’s infrastructure using legacy credentials and stole OAuth tokens connecting customers’ Salesforce environments.
  • Multiple other organizations were affected, including Recorded Future, Tanium, Jamf, Sprout Social, Gong, and Insurity.
  • LastPass disabled employee access to Klue, rotated exposed tokens, notified law enforcement, and warned users about phishing from specific sender domains.

Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT — Must Read

Malicious npm packages masquerading as PostCSS tools have been discovered delivering a multi-stage Windows remote access trojan, alongside several other supply chain attacks targeting developer credentials and Linux systems.

  • Malicious npm packages aes-decode-runner-pro, postcss-minify-selector, and postcss-minify-selector-parser deliver a Windows remote access trojan by dropping a PowerShell downloader that fetches a ZIP from nvidiadriver[.]net.
  • The RAT collects host information, steals Chrome credentials by bypassing app-bound encryption, runs shell commands, and communicates with command-and-control server 95.216.92.207:8080.
  • A separate package named apintergrationpost delivers a Linux RAT called MYRA that compiles a native C rootkit and establishes multiple persistence mechanisms.
  • The @withgoogle/stitch-sdk package steals developer credentials from eight sources including Claude Code, git config, and npm config, then exfiltrates them to stitch-production[.]org.
  • The activity overlaps with the North Korean PolinRider operation, which injects obfuscated JavaScript into GitHub repositories to distribute the BeaverTail downloader and InvisibleFerret backdoor.

Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack as hackers leak data — Should Read

Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack after the World Leaks extortion group leaked stolen data, including Apple product manufacturing schematics, while the company says operations remain unaffected.

  • Tata Electronics confirmed a cybersecurity incident on some of its IT systems but stated that its operations were not affected and continued normally.
  • The World Leaks threat group claimed the attack and leaked files that it alleged contain Apple product manufacturing data, including internal component schematics and PCB designs.
  • World Leaks is a rebrand of the Hunters International ransomware group and now operates purely as a data-extortion group, stealing files and threatening to leak them without encrypting systems.
  • Apple had not responded to BleepingComputer’s inquiry about whether any proprietary data was exposed at the time of publication.
  • Other confirmed or investigated victims of World Leaks include Dell, which confirmed a breach in July 2025, and Nike, which launched an investigation after a claimed theft of 1.4 TB of files in January 2026.

Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans and Reportedly Reached 26,000 Agents — Should Read

A fake AI agent skill passed all major security scanners by keeping the submitted package clean while hosting malicious instructions on an external link that was changed after approval, proving that scanners cannot catch runtime payload swaps.

  • AIR created a fake AI agent skill called 'brand-landingpage' that passed every tested security scanner because the submitted package contained no malicious code and directed the agent to an external link the firm controlled.
  • The skill gained credibility by having a pull request merged into a popular marketplace repository, inheriting its high GitHub star count, and was promoted via an Instagram ad.
  • After the skill was installed on roughly 26,000 agents, AIR changed the external website to serve a script that harvested email addresses, showing that scanners only evaluate a static snapshot and cannot detect changes made after approval.
  • The method reflects earlier independent research by Trail of Bits and real-world campaigns that use the same delayed-payload tactic, confirming that current skill scanners fail to address runtime content swaps.

Fake shops target shoppers across Europe with fake Samsung deals, counterfeit goods and World Cup scams — Should Read

Bitdefender Labs identified more than 55 coordinated fake-shop campaigns across 12 European countries that use Facebook ads, WhatsApp, and lookalike domains to impersonate Samsung, Nike, ZARA, and other major brands, stealing payments and personal data while exploiting FIFA World Cup 2026 excitement.

  • Bitdefender Labs uncovered more than 55 fake-shop campaigns that targeted consumers in 12 European countries between March and May 2026, impersonating brands like Samsung, Nike, Adidas, ZARA, H&M, Amazon, Lidl, and SHEIN.
  • Attackers used Facebook ads, WhatsApp messages, email, SMS, phone calls, and Unicode lookalike domains such as adidas¹.com to redirect victims to fraudulent payment or data-harvesting pages.
  • A German fake-shop network called “Homborg Online Handel” rotated domains frequently and only accepted SEPA advance bank transfers, making it nearly impossible for victims to recover their money.
  • Several campaigns exploited excitement for the FIFA World Cup 2026, offering counterfeit jerseys and fake Adidas Deutschland fan kits through redirect chains and WhatsApp messages.
  • The investigation identified a China-based operator named “Carl” who used WhatsApp to direct European users to password-protected Yupoo catalogs selling counterfeit Nike, Adidas, and luxury goods.

WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Uses Fake Documents to Install ManageEngine RMM Tool — Should Read

Attackers are hijacking WhatsApp accounts to send disguised VBScript attachments that, when opened, download secondary payloads and ultimately install the legitimate ManageEngine RMM Central tool, granting remote access to victims' systems across multiple countries.

  • Attackers sent malicious VBScript files through compromised WhatsApp accounts, disguising them as business and financial documents.
  • The campaign targets users of WhatsApp Desktop and WhatsApp Web in over ten countries, with the highest victim concentration in Malaysia.
  • The infection chain ultimately installs the legitimate ManageEngine RMM Central tool to give attackers remote access to victim systems.
  • The VBScript payloads download secondary scripts that tamper with User Account Control (UAC) behavior and deploy the RMM installer.
  • Infrastructure overlaps suggest a link to prior Gh0st RAT and ValleyRAT activity, but the campaign remains unattributed.

Five Eyes agencies sound alarm about AI’s threat to cybersecurity — Should Read

The Five Eyes intelligence alliance warns that rapidly advancing AI is dramatically shrinking the window between vulnerability discovery and exploitation, urging business leaders to urgently strengthen their cyber defenses now rather than in the coming years.

  • Five Eyes intelligence agencies (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand) warned that rapidly evolving AI is an urgent cybersecurity threat business leaders must address immediately.
  • Frontier AI models will likely exceed expectations and transform offensive and defensive cyber capabilities in months, not years.
  • AI lowers barriers for malicious actors, increases attack speed and complexity, and dramatically shrinks the time between vulnerability discovery and exploitation.
  • The agencies urged organizations to reduce attack surfaces, speed up patching, remediate unsupported legacy systems, and strengthen access controls and authentication.
  • The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency now requires federal agencies to use a triage system that can mandate patching within three days, partly based on whether AI is used to exploit a vulnerability.

VA OIG Identifies Lack of Oversight of VA GenAI Chat Tools — Should Read

The VA Office of Inspector General identified a lack of oversight and safeguards for generative AI chat tools used by staff, posing potential patient safety risks, echoing concerns highlighted in recent Health-ISAC and HSCC AI governance guidance.

  • A review by the VA Office of Inspector General found that VA staff's use of generative AI chat tools lacks safeguards and oversight, creating potential patient safety risks.
  • Health-ISAC and the Health Sector Coordinating Council Cybersecurity Working Group issued guidance earlier this year to help healthcare organizations establish effective AI governance frameworks for the safe use of GenAI tools.
  • The guidance includes a white paper titled 'Policies and Safeguards for a Safe Use of AI' and the HSCC 'Health Industry AI Cyber Governance Framework Implementation Guide.'
  • Concerns highlighted include the possibility that sensitive patient data could be shared with publicly accessible chatbots and that AI-generated output might put patients at risk of harm.

Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration — Should Read

President Trump signed an executive order requiring federal agencies to migrate key establishment to post-quantum cryptography by 2030 and digital signatures by 2031, pulling the government's prior 2035 target forward to address the 'harvest now, decrypt later' threat.

  • President Trump signed Executive Order 14409 on June 22, setting a deadline of December 31, 2030 for federal agencies to migrate key establishment to post-quantum cryptography, and December 31, 2031 for digital signatures.
  • The order directly addresses the 'harvest now, decrypt later' threat, warning that adversaries can collect encrypted U.S. data now and decrypt it later once a large-scale quantum computer is available.
  • The new deadlines pull the government’s post-quantum cryptography migration timeline forward by four to five years, moving the prior 2035 target from National Security Memorandum 10 to 2030/2031.
  • Within 30 days each agency head must name a migration lead, and within 90 days OMB must issue guidance requiring agencies to review inventories of high-value assets and high-impact systems and submit a migration plan.
  • The Federal Acquisition Regulatory Council has 180 days to propose a rule giving covered contractors until December 31, 2030 to meet NIST’s post-quantum cryptography standards, and CISA and NIST must publish minimum elements for a cryptographic bill of materials within 270 days.

Payouts King Ransomware Initial Access Broker Deploys New Edgecution Malware — Should Read

The Payouts King ransomware initial access broker deploys a novel malware, Edgecution, that uses a malicious Microsoft Edge extension abusing Chrome native messaging to escape the browser sandbox and gain full host access.

  • The attack begins with social engineering via Microsoft Teams messages impersonating a company's IT staff, directing victims to a fake Microsoft website.
  • The Edgecution malware uses a malicious Microsoft Edge browser extension that abuses Chrome native messaging to escape the browser sandbox and interact directly with the host operating system.
  • The browser extension runs in a headless Microsoft Edge instance, making it invisible to the victim and bypassing user warnings or prompts.
  • A Python-based backdoor handles core malicious actions—collecting system information, accessing the filesystem, and executing arbitrary commands—relayed from the extension via native messaging.
  • C2 communication occurs over WebSocket connections to AWS-hosted domains under cloudfront.net, with messages including command IDs for shell execution, file writing, and PowerShell execution.

OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws — Should Read

OpenAI launched its most advanced vulnerability-hunting model, GPT-5.5-Cyber, and the Patch the Planet partnership to rapidly find and fix software bugs, as AI-driven threats increasingly shrink the patching window for defenders.

  • OpenAI released GPT-5.5-Cyber, its strongest model for finding and helping patch software vulnerabilities, to trusted defenders as part of the Daybreak initiative.
  • OpenAI launched the Patch the Planet program in partnership with Trail of Bits, with initial open-source participants including cURL, NATS Server, and Python.org.
  • The Daybreak initiative has already surfaced dozens of vulnerabilities, including the HTTP/2 Bomb denial-of-service technique and multiple kernel exploits across Linux, FreeBSD, and OpenBSD.
  • Frontier AI models from OpenAI and Anthropic are accelerating vulnerability discovery, shifting the bottleneck from finding bugs to patching them and overwhelming maintainers.
  • Five Eyes intelligence agencies warned that advanced AI enables faster exploitation, shrinking the vulnerability-to-exploit timeline to months and requiring defenders to act quickly.

BioShocking AI: “Gaming” the AI Browser and Escaping its Guardrails — Should Read

Researchers developed 'BioShocking,' a technique that manipulates AI web browsers into abandoning safety guardrails by convincing them they are playing a game, successfully tricking multiple agentic browsers into leaking credentials and executing malicious actions.

  • The 'BioShocking' vulnerability manipulates AI browser agents by shifting their context into a game world, causing them to ignore built-in safety guardrails.
  • The proof-of-concept exploit successfully compromised ChatGPT Atlas, Comet, Fellou, Genspark Browser, Sigma Browser, and the Claude Chrome plugin.
  • Once tricked, the agents exfiltrated sensitive SSH credentials from an authenticated GitHub repository without recognizing the action as harmful.
  • The root cause is that AI agents trust their current context, applying game logic instead of real-world safety rules when the context is manipulated.
  • LayerX recommends that vendors add confirmation steps for sensitive operations and that users restrict the data and sessions their AI browsers can access.

Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people — Should Read

A targeted phishing attack on healthcare technology company Xsolis gave attackers access to its network, compromising sensitive personal and medical data of nearly 1.4 million individuals.

  • Xsolis detected unauthorized network activity on January 22, 2026, which originated from a targeted phishing attack on January 20, 2026.
  • The breach exposed sensitive information—including names, addresses, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, health insurance details, and medical treatment information—of almost 1.4 million individuals.
  • The company has not identified any misuse of the stolen data but is warning affected individuals to remain alert for potential targeted attacks.
  • Xsolis is mailing notification letters to affected individuals and offering 12-month identity monitoring and identity theft restoration services through Kroll.
  • In response, Xsolis reset user and account passwords, increased system monitoring, rolled out updated security measures, accelerated employee security training, and strengthened credential management mechanisms.

A One-Line Curiosity Hook Sent a K-12 Teacher to a Same-Day Phishing Domain on Port 8443 — May Read

A one-sentence curiosity lure from a legitimate foreign Microsoft 365 tenant with a display-name impersonation directed a K-12 teacher to a same-day-registered domain on non-standard port 8443, showing how attackers bypass authentication checks by using purpose-built, hours-old infrastructure.

  • The phishing email consisted solely of a one-sentence curiosity hook and a link, with no explanation of the sender or relationship, targeting a K-12 teacher in a Virginia school district.
  • The sender address was from a legitimate foreign Microsoft 365 tenant in Thailand, with a fabricated display name that impersonated a familiar person, causing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication to pass successfully.
  • The link pointed to a domain (dgfpruf[.]com) registered on the same day the email was delivered, hosted on port 8443 with a randomized subdomain and a victim-specific URL path, a pattern consistent with credential harvesting infrastructure.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the email at 51% confidence and quarantined it, based on the combination of first contact from an unrelated foreign sender, same-day domain age, non-standard port, and randomized URL structure.
  • The attacker likely obtained the recipient’s email from a public school staff directory and crafted the display name to suggest familiarity, a type of display name impersonation that bypasses technical authentication checks.

Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature — May Read

Microsoft's optional KB5095093 preview update for Windows 11 introduces a new Point-in-Time restore feature that allows quick system rollbacks, alongside fixes for the Recycle Bin bug and many other improvements.

  • The optional KB5095093 preview update introduces a Point-in-Time Restore feature that automatically creates full-system snapshots every 24 hours (for consumers) and retains them for up to 72 hours, allowing users to roll back the entire PC.
  • The update fixes a bug where permanently deleting a file from the Recycle Bin after the June 2026 security update caused the confirmation dialog to show an internal file name instead of the original file name.
  • The emoji panel now uses GIPHY for GIFs because Google's Tenor API was deprecated, and users must install this update to continue seeing GIFs in the panel.

From vulnerability report to CVE draft in minutes: how Elastic automated security advisories with AI — May Read

Elastic built a generative AI agent using Elastic Agent Builder and retrieval-augmented generation against MITRE's CWE and CAPEC catalogues indexed in Elasticsearch to automatically draft complete, accurate CVE security advisories from raw vulnerability reports, dramatically speeding up the disclosure process while grounding outputs in authoritative data.

  • Elastic built a generative AI agent using Elastic Agent Builder that drafts complete CVE security advisories from raw vulnerability reports, including CWE classification, CAPEC methodology, CVSS scoring, and mitigation guidance.
  • The agent uses retrieval-augmented generation against MITRE's CWE and CAPEC catalogues indexed in Elasticsearch via the Elastic Crawler to ground its output and prevent hallucinated classification IDs.
  • Guardrails in the system prompt, such as memory-safety verification and a minimum disclosure checklist, ensure the draft avoids sharing exploitable implementation details and aligns with Elastic's internal security practices.
  • The agent outputs both a draft advisory and a reasoning section that justifies each taxonomy and CVSS choice, enabling human reviewers to efficiently validate the results.
  • The advisory ESA-2026-01 was published as a real-world example of output that passed through this automated pipeline, proving its production readiness.

The Code You Didn't Write Is Still Yours to Defend — Optional

AI agents now routinely fetch and execute unvetted open-source packages in ephemeral sandboxes, bypassing traditional supply chain controls, so organizations must adopt real-time threat intelligence at the point of ingest to defend against attacks that weaponize within minutes.

  • AI assistants now fetch and execute open source packages in sandboxes without human review, creating a software supply chain event that traditional scanners and proxies miss.
  • Attackers can use a simple prompt to generate code that appears routine yet includes a backdoor, and working exploits can emerge within two hours of a patch release.
  • A cooldown that blocks packages newer than seven days also delays security patches, widening the exposure because the patch-to-exploit window is now only hours.
  • Governance at the point of ingest, using an hourly updated threat intelligence feed that blocks downloads or builds when a malicious package appears, is the effective defense.
  • The agent environment is the fastest growing blind spot, and Socket Firewall addresses this by protecting endpoints where code runs without a build pipeline.

Measuring Identity Risk: The Metrics Every Security Leader Should Track — Optional

Identity risk is an under-measured but critical attack surface; security leaders should track metrics like total exposed identities, executive exposure, and exposure velocity to quantify risk, prioritize remediation, and communicate effectively to the board.

  • Identity has become a primary attack surface, yet many organizations lack meaningful metrics to measure identity risk.
  • The article defines eight specific identity risk metrics, including Total Exposed Identities, High-Risk Identity Exposure Rate, Executive Exposure Score, and Exposure Velocity.
  • Measuring identity risk enables security leaders to prioritize investments, focus remediation, communicate risk to leadership, and demonstrate progress.
  • Boards are increasingly asking for identity risk metrics, such as executive and third-party exposure, because traditional cybersecurity metrics fail to answer these questions.
  • Identity risk is dynamic, persistent, and distributed; exposed credentials can remain usable indefinitely, making identity risk uniquely difficult to quantify.

Compromise kids online safety bill unveiled by House leaders, with key omission — Optional

The House released a bipartisan kids online safety bill that notably excludes a 'duty of care' provision requiring platforms to mitigate harms like cyberbullying and eating disorders, a decision that may jeopardize support from many lawmakers and advocates.

  • The House bill released by the Energy and Commerce Committee excludes a 'duty of care' provision that would have required online platforms to modify algorithms and design to prevent harms like suicidal ideation, eating disorders, and cyberbullying.
  • The compromise package blocks minors from using disappearing messages, mandates AI chatbots to acknowledge they are not human, forces porn sites to use age verification technology, and establishes a data broker registry for children's data.
  • Senator Marsha Blackburn criticized the omission, and 76 senators have previously supported including a duty of care in the legislation; her own forthcoming proposal with the White House is expected to retain that standard.
  • Kate Ruane of the Center for Democracy and Technology warned that the bill incentivizes age verification, putting the privacy of all internet users at risk and threatening access to constitutionally protected materials.
  • The new House version aligns more closely with the Senate bill, but Senate Democrats have indicated that passing the bill without a duty of care will be difficult.

Aikido x Drydock | A way for maintainers to catch malware before it ships — Optional

Aikido and Drydock are launching a free integration that lets npm and PyPI maintainers inspect and diff staged package releases before publishing, catching malware during approval rather than after it ships.

  • Aikido and Drydock partnered to give npm and PyPI maintainers a free way to inspect the exact contents of a staged package release before it publishes.
  • Drydock diffs a staged release against the last published version and flags security-relevant changes like new lifecycle scripts, network access, or added binaries, ranked by severity.
  • For npm staged publishing, an approving maintainer can see the changed bytes and then approve publish with their own 2FA; for PyPI or unstaged npm, Drydock runs as a GitHub Actions gate.
  • Drydock uses a read-only token, never runs the package contents, never publishes itself, and keeps a human in the loop for every approval decision.
  • The goal is to catch malware during the approval stage instead of after it ships, breaking the pattern of post-mortem malware disclosures and damage control.

Webinar: Why email security teams are drowning in alerts — Optional

A July 8 webinar will address how behavioral AI can automate email security workflows to reduce alert fatigue and accelerate investigation and response to phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks.

  • A live webinar titled 'Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI' will be held on July 8, 2026, hosted by BleepingComputer.
  • The webinar is presented by Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • Security teams face alert fatigue and investigation backlogs due to the high volume of manual tasks required to investigate phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks.
  • The session examines how behavioral AI can automate detection, investigation, and remediation workflows to surface the highest-risk incidents and accelerate responses.
  • Attendees will learn practical techniques to reduce manual analysis, prioritize high-risk threats, and improve operational efficiency in email security operations.

The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You — Optional

You can prove a vulnerability is not exploitable in your environment without a live exploit by validating each technique in its attack chain against your existing controls, giving you a defensible verdict even for day-one CVEs or off-limits assets.

  • The average time from vulnerability disclosure to exploitation has fallen to roughly 8 hours in 2026, while median fix times for known-exploited vulnerabilities have risen to 43 days.
  • Automated penetration testing is limited because it cannot safely test assets that are business-critical, air-gapped, or lack a public exploit, leaving 85-90% of an enterprise's exposures unaddressed.
  • TTP-chain validation breaks a vulnerability into the sequence of attacker techniques required for exploitation and tests each step against existing security controls, providing a defensible verdict without launching a live exploit.
  • The technique-chain approach works on day one of a disclosure, on assets where live exploits are unsafe, and even when no working exploit exists, because it validates by inference rather than detonation.
  • CVE-2025-29824, a Windows CLFS use-after-free exploited by Storm-2460 in RansomEXX attacks, is used as an example of how TTP-chain validation can determine exploitability by testing individual techniques like MSBuild execution and LSASS dumping against controls.

Agentic AI: The Weapon That No Longer Needs a Warrior — Optional

Agentic AI allows offensive tools to plan and execute attacks without human guidance, lowering the barrier for unskilled attackers and eroding traditional detection signals, which makes human oversight and verification the critical remaining role.

  • Agentic AI autonomously executes entire attack chains, eliminating the need for a human operator to guide each step.
  • This technology grants unskilled attackers real offensive capability, a phenomenon the author terms 'script kiddie as a service'.
  • Autonomous social engineering erases classic detection signals by generating fluent, personalized messages that do not follow mass-produced templates.
  • AI-driven exploit chains can produce false conclusions with high confidence because agents match indicators to assumptions without verifying actual target conditions.
  • Human oversight becomes the critical component, as the machine can aim itself but cannot judge whether an attack should be launched.

Silent Push partners with CDL Cyber Intelligence to bring preemptive defense to the United Kingdom — Optional

Silent Push and CDL Cyber Intelligence have partnered to provide UK organizations with early warning of cyber threats by detecting adversary staging infrastructure, offering an average 157-day lead time before an attack hits their perimeter.

  • Silent Push and CDL Cyber Intelligence have partnered to deliver preemptive defense by uncovering adversary staging infrastructure long before an attack launches.
  • The Context Graph continuously monitors DNS, WHOIS, certificate, and hosting data to surface clusters of preparation activity as Indicators of Future Attack.
  • Clients of CDL gain an average of 157 days of warning before a threat campaign reaches their network perimeter.
  • The UK is a high-risk target for state-linked threat actors due to its roles as a NATO member, financial hub, and home to critical infrastructure.
  • Traffic Origin technology reveals the true upstream country controlling a connection, even when attackers mask it with local-looking residential proxies or VPNs.
